topics.title: What is Python?
topics.content:
What is Python?

Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language known for its simple and readable syntax. It was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991.

Python is widely used in:

  • 🌐 Web development (Django, Flask)
  • 🤖 Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning
  • 📊 Data Science & Analytics
  • 🔧 Automation & Scripting
  • 🎮 Game Development

Why Python?

Python's philosophy emphasizes code readability. It reads almost like plain English, making it one of the best first languages for beginners.

# Python — clean and simple
print("Hello, World!")

topics.title: Your First Python Program
topics.content:
Hello, World!

Every programming journey starts with "Hello, World!". In Python, it's just one line:

print("Hello, World!")

The print() function outputs text to the screen.

More Examples

print("My name is Python!")
print("I am learning to code.")
print("2 + 2 =", 2 + 2)

Comments

Comments are notes Python ignores. Use #:

# This is a comment
print("This runs!")  # inline comment

topics.title: Python Syntax Basics
topics.content:
Indentation

Python uses indentation to define code blocks — this is mandatory.

if True:
    print("Indented correctly")
    print("Same block")

Case Sensitivity

name, Name, and NAME are three different variables.

name = "Alice"
Name = "Bob"
print(name)  # Alice
print(Name)  # Bob

Statements

Each statement goes on its own line. No semicolons needed.

x = 5
y = 10
print(x + y)  # 15

evalQuestions.question: Which function is used to display output in Python?
What symbol is used to write a comment in Python?
Python was created by ___.
What will `print(2 + 3)` output?

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
What is a Variable?

A variable is a named container that stores a value.

name = "Alice"      # string
age  = 20           # integer
height = 5.6        # float
is_student = True   # boolean

Naming Rules

  • Must start with a letter or underscore
  • Can contain letters, numbers, underscores
  • Cannot use reserved keywords (if, for, etc.)
  • Case-sensitive: score ≠ Score
# Valid
student_name = "Bob"
_count = 42
totalScore = 95

# Invalid — causes errors
2fast = 100      # starts with number
my-var = "hi"   # hyphens not allowed

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
Core Data Types

  • int — whole numbers: 5, -3, 100
  • float — decimals: 3.14, -0.5
  • str — text: "hello", 'Python'
  • bool — True or False
x = 42          # int
y = 3.14        # float
z = "Python"    # str
flag = True     # bool

print(type(x))  # <class 'int'>
print(type(y))  # <class 'float'>

topics.title: Type Conversion
topics.content:
Converting Between Types

x = "42"       # string
y = int(x)     # → integer: 42
z = float(x)   # → float: 42.0
w = str(100)   # → string: "100"

print(y + 8)   # 50
print(w + " pts")  # "100 pts"

Common Mistake with input()

age = input("Enter your age: ")  # returns string!
age = int(age)                    # convert first
next_year = age + 1
print("Next year:", next_year)

evalQuestions.question: Which data type stores the value 3.14?
What does `type(True)` return?
What will `int("7") + 3` evaluate to?
What function converts a value to a string?

topics.title: Arithmetic Operators
topics.content:
Arithmetic Operators

a = 10
b = 3

print(a + b)   # 13  Addition
print(a - b)   # 7   Subtraction
print(a * b)   # 30  Multiplication
print(a / b)   # 3.333 Division (always float)
print(a // b)  # 3   Floor Division
print(a % b)   # 1   Modulus (remainder)
print(a ** b)  # 1000 Exponentiation

Order of Operations (PEMDAS)

result = 2 + 3 * 4      # 14 (not 20)
result = (2 + 3) * 4   # 20 (parentheses first)

topics.title: Comparison & Logical Operators
topics.content:
Comparison Operators

Return True or False:

x = 10
y = 5
print(x == y)  # False
print(x != y)  # True
print(x > y)   # True
print(x < y)   # False
print(x >= 10) # True

Logical Operators

age = 20
has_id = True

print(age >= 18 and has_id)  # True (both must be True)
print(age < 18 or has_id)    # True (at least one True)
print(not has_id)             # False (reverses)

topics.title: Assignment Operators
topics.content:
Shorthand Assignment

score = 100
score += 10   # score = score + 10 → 110
score -= 5    # → 105
score *= 2    # → 210
score //= 3   # → 70
score **= 2   # → 4900
score %= 100  # → 0

print(score)  # 0

evalQuestions.question: What does `10 % 3` evaluate to?
What is `2 ** 4`?
What does `x += 5` mean?
Which operator checks equality?

topics.title: if / else
topics.content:
Making Decisions

score = 65

if score >= 70:
    print("You passed!")
else:
    print("Keep practicing.")

elif — Multiple Conditions

grade = 85

if grade >= 90:
    print("A — Excellent!")
elif grade >= 80:
    print("B — Great job!")
elif grade >= 70:
    print("C — Good work.")
else:
    print("Needs improvement.")

topics.title: Nested Conditions & Ternary
topics.content:
Nested if

age = 20
has_ticket = True

if age >= 18:
    if has_ticket:
        print("You may enter.")
    else:
        print("You need a ticket.")
else:
    print("Must be 18+.")

One-Line Ternary

result = "Pass" if score >= 70 else "Fail"
print(result)

# Even/odd check
parity = "even" if 7 % 2 == 0 else "odd"
print(parity)  # odd

evalQuestions.question: What keyword is used for a secondary condition after `if`?
What will this print? `x = 10; if x > 5: print('big') else: print('small')`
`"even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd"` — result when n = 4?

topics.title: while Loop
topics.content:
The while Loop

Repeats while a condition is True:

count = 1
while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1
# Prints: 1 2 3 4 5

break and continue

i = 0
while True:
    i += 1
    if i == 3: continue  # skip 3
    if i > 5:  break     # stop at 6
    print(i)             # 1 2 4 5

topics.title: for Loop & range()
topics.content:
for Loop

for i in range(5):
    print(i)  # 0 1 2 3 4

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

range() Variants

range(5)         # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
range(1, 6)      # 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
range(0, 10, 2)  # 0, 2, 4, 6, 8
range(10, 0, -1) # 10, 9, ... 1

enumerate()

names = ["Ana", "Ben", "Cara"]
for i, name in enumerate(names):
    print(f"{i}: {name}")

evalQuestions.question: How many times does `for i in range(3)` loop?
What keyword immediately exits a loop?
What does `range(2, 8, 2)` produce?
Which keyword skips the current iteration?

topics.title: Defining Functions
topics.content:
What is a Function?

A reusable block of code that performs a specific task.

def greet():
    print("Hello, welcome!")

greet()  # Call it anytime

Parameters

def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Alice")  # Hello, Alice!
greet("Bob")    # Hello, Bob!

topics.title: Return Values & Defaults
topics.content:
Returning Data

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(5, 3)
print(result)  # 8

Default Parameters

def power(base, exponent=2):
    return base ** exponent

print(power(3))     # 9  (default used)
print(power(3, 3))  # 27

Multiple Return Values

def min_max(nums):
    return min(nums), max(nums)

lo, hi = min_max([4, 1, 9, 2])
print(lo, hi)  # 1 9

topics.title: Scope
topics.content:
Local vs Global Variables

def my_func():
    local_var = 10
    print(local_var)  # Works

my_func()
# print(local_var)  # NameError!

global Keyword

counter = 0

def increment():
    global counter
    counter += 1

increment()
increment()
print(counter)  # 2

evalQuestions.question: What keyword defines a function?
What does `double(6)` return if `def double(x): return x * 2`?
A variable created inside a function is called ___.
What keyword sends a value back from a function?

topics.title: Lists
topics.content:
Working with Lists

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

print(fruits[0])    # apple
print(fruits[-1])   # cherry
print(fruits[1:3])  # ['banana', 'cherry']

fruits.append("mango")
fruits.insert(1, "orange")
fruits.remove("banana")
popped = fruits.pop()
print(len(fruits))

topics.title: Tuples
topics.content:
Immutable Sequences

point = (3, 7)
rgb   = (255, 128, 0)

print(point[0])  # 3
print(len(rgb))  # 3
# point[0] = 10  # TypeError — immutable!

Tuple Unpacking

x, y = (10, 20)
print(x)  # 10
print(y)  # 20

evalQuestions.question: Which method adds an item to the end of a list?
What is the index of the first element?
What makes a tuple different from a list?

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.content:
Key-Value Storage

student = {
    "name": "Alice",
    "age": 20,
    "grade": "A"
}

print(student["name"])     # Alice
print(student.get("age"))  # 20

student["score"] = 95  # Add
student["grade"] = "A+" # Update
del student["age"]      # Delete

topics.title: Dict Methods & Sets
topics.content:
Dictionary Methods

person = {"name": "Bob", "age": 30}

print(person.keys())    # dict_keys(['name', 'age'])
print(person.values())  # dict_values(['Bob', 30])

for k, v in person.items():
    print(f"{k}: {v}")

Sets — Unique Values

colors = {"red", "green", "blue", "red"}
print(colors)  # no duplicates!

a = {1, 2, 3, 4}
b = {3, 4, 5, 6}
print(a | b)  # Union
print(a & b)  # Intersection: {3, 4}
print(a - b)  # Difference: {1, 2}

evalQuestions.question: How do you access key "name" in dict `d`?
What does a set guarantee?
Which method returns all key-value pairs as tuples?

topics.title: Common String Methods
topics.content:
String Manipulation

text = "  Hello, Python World!  "

print(text.strip())          # Remove spaces
print(text.lower())          # lowercase
print(text.upper())          # UPPERCASE
print(text.replace("Python", "Amazing"))
print(text.count("l"))       # 3
print(text.find("Python"))   # index

words = "the quick fox".split()
print(words)  # ['the', 'quick', 'fox']
print("-".join(words))  # the-quick-fox

topics.title: f-strings & Slicing
topics.content:
f-strings

name  = "Alice"
score = 95.5

print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"Score: {score:.1f}%")
print(f"2 + 2 = {2 + 2}")

Slicing

text = "Hello, World!"
print(text[0:5])    # Hello
print(text[7:])     # World!
print(text[::-1])   # reversed

evalQuestions.question: What does `"hello".upper()` return?
Which f-string embeds variable `x = 5` correctly?
What does `text[::-1]` do?

topics.title: Reading & Writing Files
topics.content:
Opening Files

# Read
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)

# Write (overwrite)
with open("output.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello, File!\n")

# Append
with open("output.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("Another line\n")

💡 Use with — it automatically closes the file.

evalQuestions.question: Which mode appends without deleting existing content?
What is the advantage of `with open(...) as f`?
Which mode overwrites a file completely?

topics.title: Classes & Objects
topics.content:
OOP Basics

class Dog:
    species = "Canis familiaris"

    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age  = age

    def bark(self):
        return f"{self.name} says: Woof!"

dog1 = Dog("Rex", 3)
print(dog1.bark())   # Rex says: Woof!
print(dog1.name)     # Rex

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.content:
Child Classes

class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    def speak(self):
        return "..."

class Cat(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name}: Meow!"

class Duck(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name}: Quack!"

for a in [Cat("Whiskers"), Duck("Donald")]:
    print(a.speak())

evalQuestions.question: What special method is called when creating a new object?
In `class Cat(Animal):`, what is `Animal`?
What does `self` refer to in a class method?

topics.title: try / except / finally
topics.content:
Handling Errors

try:
    n = int(input("Enter a number: "))
    print(10 / n)
except ValueError:
    print("Not a valid number!")
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Cannot divide by zero!")
finally:
    print("Always runs.")

Raising Exceptions

def set_age(age):
    if age < 0:
        raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative!")
    return age

evalQuestions.question: Which block always executes regardless of exceptions?
What exception is raised when dividing by zero?
What keyword deliberately triggers an exception?

topics.title: Comprehensions
topics.content:
List Comprehensions

squares = [x ** 2 for x in range(1, 6)]
print(squares)  # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)    # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

Dict Comprehensions

words = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
lengths = {w: len(w) for w in words}
print(lengths)  # {'apple': 5, ...}

topics.title: Lambda, map & filter
topics.content:
Lambda Functions

double = lambda x: x * 2
print(double(5))   # 10

add = lambda a, b: a + b
print(add(3, 7))   # 10

map() and filter()

nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

doubled = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, nums))
print(doubled)  # [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12]

evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print(evens)    # [2, 4, 6]

evalQuestions.question: What does `[x**2 for x in range(4)]` produce?
What does `lambda x: x * 3` do?
options: Creates an anonymous function that triples x
evalQuestions.correctAnswer: Creates an anonymous function that triples x

evalQuestions.question: Which built-in applies a function to every element of an iterable?

topics.title: What is JavaScript?
topics.content:
What is JavaScript?

JavaScript (JS) is the programming language of the web. It runs in the browser and makes web pages interactive.

  • 🌐 Runs natively in every browser
  • ⚡ Makes pages dynamic (popups, animations, live updates)
  • 🖥️ Also runs on servers via Node.js
  • 📱 Used for mobile apps (React Native)

Your First JS Program

console.log("Hello, World!");
console.log(2 + 3);       // 5
console.log("2 + 3 =", 2 + 3); // 2 + 3 = 5

console.log() is the JavaScript equivalent of Python's print().

topics.title: Variables — let, const, var
topics.content:
Declaring Variables

JavaScript has three ways to declare variables:

let   name = "Alice";    // can be reassigned
const PI   = 3.14159;    // cannot be reassigned
var   age  = 20;         // old-style, avoid in modern JS

let vs const

let score = 85;
score = 90;     // OK — let allows reassignment

const MAX = 100;
// MAX = 200;   // TypeError — const is read-only

// Best practice: use const by default, let when you need to change

Naming Rules

  • Start with a letter, _, or $
  • Case-sensitive: name ≠ Name
  • Use camelCase: firstName, totalScore

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
JavaScript Data Types

let num     = 42;           // Number
let price   = 9.99;         // Number (no separate float)
let name    = "Alice";      // String
let isReady = true;         // Boolean
let nothing = null;         // Null (intentional empty)
let undef;                  // Undefined (not assigned yet)

console.log(typeof num);     // "number"
console.log(typeof name);    // "string"
console.log(typeof isReady); // "boolean"

Type Coercion

console.log("5" + 3);   // "53"  (string concat!)
console.log("5" - 3);   // 2     (numeric subtraction)
console.log(Number("42")); // 42
console.log(String(100));  // "100"

evalQuestions.question: Which function logs output to the browser console in JavaScript?
Which keyword declares a variable that CANNOT be reassigned?
What does `typeof "hello"` return?
What does `"5" + 3` evaluate to in JavaScript?

topics.title: Arithmetic & Assignment
topics.content:
Arithmetic Operators

let a = 10, b = 3;

console.log(a + b);   // 13
console.log(a - b);   // 7
console.log(a * b);   // 30
console.log(a / b);   // 3.333...
console.log(a % b);   // 1  (remainder)
console.log(a ** b);  // 1000 (exponentiation)

Assignment Operators

let x = 10;
x += 5;   // 15
x -= 3;   // 12
x *= 2;   // 24
x /= 4;   // 6
x++;      // 7  (increment)
x--;      // 6  (decrement)

topics.title: Comparison & Logical
topics.content:
Comparison Operators

console.log(5 == "5");   // true  (loose, coerces types)
console.log(5 === "5");  // false (strict, no coercion)
console.log(5 !== 3);    // true
console.log(10 > 5);     // true
console.log(10 <= 10);   // true

💡 Always prefer === over == in JavaScript.

Logical Operators

let age = 20;
let hasId = true;

console.log(age >= 18 && hasId);  // true  (AND)
console.log(age < 18 || hasId);   // true  (OR)
console.log(!hasId);               // false (NOT)

topics.title: Template Literals
topics.content:
Template Literals

Use backticks (`) for string interpolation — like Python's f-strings:

const name  = "Alice";
const score = 95;

console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`);
console.log(`Score: ${score}%`);
console.log(`2 + 2 = ${2 + 2}`);

// Multi-line strings
const msg = `
  Welcome to JavaScript!
  Today we learn operators.
`;
console.log(msg);

evalQuestions.question: What does `10 % 3` evaluate to?
Which operator checks both value AND type equality?
What syntax is used for string interpolation in template literals?
`x++` is shorthand for ___.

topics.title: if / else / switch
topics.content:
if / else if / else

const grade = 85;

if (grade >= 90) {
    console.log("A — Excellent!");
} else if (grade >= 80) {
    console.log("B — Great job!");
} else if (grade >= 70) {
    console.log("C — Good work.");
} else {
    console.log("Needs improvement.");
}

switch Statement

const day = "Monday";

switch (day) {
    case "Monday":
        console.log("Start of work week");
        break;
    case "Friday":
        console.log("TGIF!");
        break;
    default:
        console.log("Midweek");
}

topics.title: Loops
topics.content:
for Loop

for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    console.log(i);  // 0 1 2 3 4
}

const fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"];
for (const fruit of fruits) {
    console.log(fruit);
}

while Loop

let count = 1;
while (count <= 5) {
    console.log(count);
    count++;
}

forEach

fruits.forEach((fruit, index) => {
    console.log(`${index}: ${fruit}`);
});

evalQuestions.question: What keyword is needed in a `switch` to prevent fall-through?
Which loop iterates over items in an array using `of`?
In `for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++)`, how many iterations?

topics.title: Function Declaration & Expression
topics.content:
Function Declaration

function greet(name) {
    return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}

console.log(greet("Alice"));  // Hello, Alice!

Function Expression

const add = function(a, b) {
    return a + b;
};

console.log(add(3, 5));  // 8

Default Parameters

function power(base, exponent = 2) {
    return base ** exponent;
}

console.log(power(3));     // 9
console.log(power(3, 3));  // 27

topics.title: Arrow Functions
topics.content:
Arrow Function Syntax

A concise way to write functions, introduced in ES6:

// Traditional
function double(x) {
    return x * 2;
}

// Arrow function
const double = (x) => x * 2;
const double = x => x * 2;   // parens optional for 1 param

console.log(double(5));  // 10

Multi-line Arrow Function

const add = (a, b) => {
    const sum = a + b;
    return sum;
};

// Commonly used with array methods:
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const squared = nums.map(n => n ** 2);
console.log(squared);  // [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

evalQuestions.question: Which is a valid arrow function?
What does this return? `const add = (a, b) => a + b; add(4, 6)`
What keyword is used to send a value back from a function?

topics.title: Array Methods
topics.content:
Essential Array Methods

const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

// map — transform each element
const doubled = nums.map(n => n * 2);
console.log(doubled);  // [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]

// filter — keep matching elements
const evens = nums.filter(n => n % 2 === 0);
console.log(evens);    // [2, 4]

// reduce — accumulate to a single value
const sum = nums.reduce((acc, n) => acc + n, 0);
console.log(sum);      // 15

// find — first match
const first = nums.find(n => n > 3);
console.log(first);    // 4

topics.title: Spread & Destructuring
topics.content:
Array Destructuring

const [first, second, ...rest] = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50];
console.log(first);   // 10
console.log(second);  // 20
console.log(rest);    // [30, 40, 50]

Spread Operator

const a = [1, 2, 3];
const b = [4, 5, 6];
const combined = [...a, ...b];  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

// Copy an array
const copy = [...a];  // [1, 2, 3] — independent copy

// Math with spread
console.log(Math.max(...a));  // 3

evalQuestions.question: Which method creates a new array by transforming each element?
What does `[1,2,3].filter(n => n > 1)` return?
What does `[...a, ...b]` do?

topics.title: Object Literals
topics.content:
Creating Objects

const student = {
    name: "Alice",
    age: 20,
    grade: "A",
    greet() {
        return `Hi, I'm ${this.name}`;
    }
};

console.log(student.name);       // Alice
console.log(student["age"]);     // 20
console.log(student.greet());    // Hi, I'm Alice

student.score = 95;  // Add property
delete student.age;  // Remove property

topics.title: Object Destructuring
topics.content:
Destructuring Objects

const person = { name: "Bob", age: 30, city: "Manila" };

const { name, age } = person;
console.log(name);  // Bob
console.log(age);   // 30

// Rename while destructuring
const { name: fullName, city: location } = person;
console.log(fullName);  // Bob
console.log(location);  // Manila

Spread with Objects

const base = { theme: "dark", lang: "en" };
const user = { ...base, name: "Alice", lang: "fil" };
console.log(user);
// { theme: 'dark', lang: 'fil', name: 'Alice' }

evalQuestions.question: What does `this` refer to inside an object method?
What does `const { name } = person` do?
How do you add a property `score = 100` to object `obj`?

topics.title: Selecting & Modifying Elements
topics.content:
Selecting Elements

// By ID
const title = document.getElementById("main-title");

// By CSS selector
const btn    = document.querySelector(".submit-btn");
const items  = document.querySelectorAll("li");

// Modifying content
title.textContent = "New Title";
title.innerHTML   = "Bold Title";

// Modifying style
title.style.color     = "red";
title.style.fontSize  = "24px";

// Modifying attributes
btn.setAttribute("disabled", true);
btn.classList.add("active");
btn.classList.remove("hidden");

topics.title: Event Listeners
topics.content:
Handling Events

const btn = document.querySelector("#myBtn");

btn.addEventListener("click", function() {
    console.log("Button clicked!");
});

// Arrow function
btn.addEventListener("click", () => {
    btn.textContent = "Clicked!";
    btn.style.background = "green";
});

// Other events
document.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => {
    console.log("Key pressed:", e.key);
});

input.addEventListener("input", (e) => {
    console.log("Value:", e.target.value);
});

evalQuestions.question: Which method selects the first element matching a CSS selector?
Which method attaches an event handler to an element?
What property changes the visible text inside an element (no HTML parsing)?

topics.title: Promises
topics.content:
What is a Promise?

A Promise represents a value that will be available in the future (async operation).

const promise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const success = true;
    if (success) {
        resolve("Data loaded!");
    } else {
        reject("Something went wrong.");
    }
});

promise
    .then(data  => console.log(data))   // success
    .catch(err  => console.log(err))    // failure
    .finally(() => console.log("Done")); // always

topics.title: async / await & Fetch
topics.content:
async/await

A cleaner syntax for working with Promises:

async function fetchUser(id) {
    try {
        const response = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
        const data     = await response.json();
        console.log(data.name);
    } catch (error) {
        console.error("Failed:", error);
    }
}

fetchUser(1);

Fetch API

// POST request
async function createPost(title, body) {
    const res = await fetch("/api/posts", {
        method: "POST",
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: JSON.stringify({ title, body })
    });
    return await res.json();
}

evalQuestions.question: Which keyword pauses execution until a Promise resolves?
What does a Promise's `.catch()` handler do?
A function that uses `await` must be declared with ___.

topics.title: Classes & Inheritance
topics.content:
JavaScript Classes

class Animal {
    constructor(name) {
        this.name = name;
    }
    speak() {
        return `${this.name} makes a sound.`;
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    speak() {
        return `${this.name} barks.`;
    }
}

const d = new Dog("Rex");
console.log(d.speak());       // Rex barks.
console.log(d instanceof Animal); // true

topics.title: Modules (import / export)
topics.content:
ES6 Modules

// math.js — named exports
export function add(a, b)  { return a + b; }
export function sub(a, b)  { return a - b; }
export const PI = 3.14159;

// Default export
export default function multiply(a, b) { return a * b; }
// main.js — importing
import multiply from "./math.js";
import { add, PI } from "./math.js";
import * as MathUtils from "./math.js";

console.log(add(3, 5));        // 8
console.log(PI);               // 3.14159
console.log(multiply(4, 5));   // 20
console.log(MathUtils.sub(10, 3)); // 7

evalQuestions.question: Which keyword inherits from a parent class?
What keyword imports the default export from a module?
What is `constructor()` used for in a class?

topics.title: try / catch / finally
topics.content:
Error Handling

try {
    const data = JSON.parse(invalidJson);
} catch (error) {
    console.error("Parse failed:", error.message);
} finally {
    console.log("Cleanup done.");
}

// Throwing custom errors
function divide(a, b) {
    if (b === 0) throw new Error("Division by zero!");
    return a / b;
}

try {
    divide(10, 0);
} catch (e) {
    console.log(e.message);  // Division by zero!
}

topics.title: Closures
topics.content:
What is a Closure?

A closure is a function that remembers the variables from its outer scope even after the outer function has returned.

function makeCounter() {
    let count = 0;        // closed-over variable
    return function() {
        count++;
        return count;
    };
}

const counter = makeCounter();
console.log(counter());  // 1
console.log(counter());  // 2
console.log(counter());  // 3

Practical Use — Factory

function multiplier(factor) {
    return (num) => num * factor;
}

const double = multiplier(2);
const triple = multiplier(3);
console.log(double(5));  // 10
console.log(triple(5));  // 15

evalQuestions.question: Which block runs regardless of whether an error occurred?
What is a closure?
options: A function that remembers its outer scope variables
A way to close a file
evalQuestions.correctAnswer: A function that remembers its outer scope variables
